summary: Jess Frazelle describes building an automated internal infrastructure system at Oxide Computer Company that consolidates data from various external services (Google Forms, GitHub, etc.) into a single database with an API layer on top. The system evolved from 'bash, popsicle sticks, glue, and some rust' into a cleaner Rust-based architecture that provides resilience against API rate limits and service downtime. The infrastructure handles everything from applicant tracking to RFD (Request for Discussion) document management, with Airtable serving as a visual frontend for the data.
key_points: Built a centralized database to cache data from external services, protecting against rate limits and downtime
Automated applicant tracking including document parsing, email notifications, and GitHub issue creation for onboarding
RFD content stored in database enables easy searching and sharing with external collaborators
API server acts as read-only middle-man between database and internal tools/users
Airtable used as visual frontend with automatic syncing from the database
money_quotes: I joke so much that I automated our CIO that I even named the repo holding the code…cio.
Previously, our infrastructure was held together with bash, popsicle sticks, glue, and some rust. Now, it is mostly rust and a much more sane architecture to grok.
We also get the freedom of caching all our data in a database that we own so we can access it even when services are down.
At this point, I can't imagine working at a company without an internal API for querying everything from Google groups, to applicants, to mailing list subscribers, to RFDs, and more.
All in all, this was pretty fun to build, refactor, build, and refactor again.
key_insight: Owning your company's operational data in a centralized database with an API layer provides resilience, searchability, and the flexibility to build custom tooling on top of external services.
